expertise_area: AI, reinforcement learning, and post-training of language models

key_insights.insight: Pre-training teaches models to imitate web content and assign probabilities, while post-training narrows behavior to a helpful chat assistant optimizing for human preference.
key_insights.quote: In pre-training, you're basically training to imitate all of the content on the internet or on the web... And then when we do post-training, we're usually targeting a narrower range of behavior where we basically want the model to behave like this kind of chat assistant... optimizing on a different objective, which is more about producing outputs that humans will like and find useful as opposed to just trying to imitate this raw content from the web.

key_insights.insight: Long horizon RL training enables models to act coherently over extended periods, allowing them to carry out complex tasks like multi-file coding projects and recover from errors via generalization.
key_insights.quote: So you could imagine having the models carry out a whole coding project... and test it, look at the output, iterate on that a bit... the unlock is that it can act coherently for long enough to write multiple files of code... Also, I would expect that as the models get better, they're just better at recovering from errors... they know how to recover from it.

key_insights.insight: Models fine-tuned in one language generalize to behave helpfully in other languages without explicit multilingual training.
key_insights.quote: if you do all your fine-tuning with English data, you'll automatically, you'll have the model also behaving well in other languages... you get it to respond in Spanish to Spanish queries.

key_insights.insight: Post-training can yield large performance gains (e.g., a 100‑point ELO increase) and remains a promising avenue for further improvement.
key_insights.quote: The current GPD4 has a ELO score that is like a hundred points higher than the original one that was released... I would say that most of that is post-training.

key_insights.insight: Beyond coherence, models may lack human-like taste, ambiguity handling, and face mundane barriers such as UI affordances that could slow progress.
key_insights.quote: Maybe there's some other experience that human experts bring to different tasks, like having some taste or dealing with ambiguity better... there might be a lot of like mundane barriers that are probably not going to last that long, but would initially like slow down progress.

topics: AI safety coordination and gradual deployment
multimodal AI and vision‑based web use

actionable_advice.advice: Use long horizon RL training to teach models to complete multi‑step coding projects.
actionable_advice.how_to: Train on sequences of actions (write, test, iterate) with rewards for final correct output, gradually increasing horizon length.

actionable_advice.advice: Implement coordinated safety checks and gradual deployment when approaching advanced AI capabilities.
actionable_advice.how_to: Set up shared evaluation frameworks, run red‑team simulations, and monitor deployed systems for early warning signs before scaling up.

actionable_advice.advice: Leverage cross‑lingual generalization to reduce language‑specific fine‑tuning effort.
actionable_advice.how_to: Fine‑tune primarily on high‑quality English data and validate behavior in target languages; rely on the model’s transfer ability.

contrarian_claims.claim: Improving long‑horizon coherence alone will not lead to AGI; other bottlenecks like taste and ambiguity remain.
contrarian_claims.context: Many expect that solving coherence will unlock human‑level agency, but Schulman argues models will still lack higher‑level judgment and face practical barriers.

contrarian_claims.claim: Future AI systems will be able to use existing human‑designed websites without needing AI‑specific APIs or redesigns.
contrarian_claims.context: Contrary to the idea that new UIs must be built for AI, Schulman believes improved vision will let models interact with current web interfaces.
